misconceptionSummary: Students think base 10 is the only 'real' number system — they treat other bases as tricks rather than genuine place value systems.

visuals.ruleText: Every place value system works identically — columns are powers of the base, digits range from 0 to base−1
visuals.subText: Base 10: columns are 10⁰, 10¹, 10²… Base 2: columns are 2⁰, 2¹, 2²…

steps.narration: All place value systems share the same structure. The base is the only thing that changes. Swap ten for two and you get binary — a perfectly consistent number system using only the digits zero and one.

lines.text: Computers use base 2 — circuits are on or off
Clocks use base 60 for minutes and seconds

steps.narration: Different bases suit different purposes. Binary matches electronic switches — on or off, one or zero. Hexadecimal is shorthand for binary in computing. Base sixty has been used for time since ancient Babylon.
The column values change with the base, but the logic is identical: each column to the left is the base times bigger than the one to its right.

visuals.label: Any base: digit × column power → sum the results

steps.narration: One zero one in binary: one four, no twos, one one — equals five in base ten. The method is identical to base ten expansion, just with powers of two.

misconceptionStrip.text: Common mistake: thinking base 10 is uniquely 'correct'. Every base is a valid place value system with the same internal logic — only the column sizes differ.
misconceptionStrip.audioNarration: Base 10 is not special — it is just the most familiar. Any base works: columns are powers of the base, digits range from 0 to base minus 1.
